title: Privatisation, nationalisation & regulation
summary:
## The big idea Unit 7 showed *why* market power hurts consumers; this chapter is about what the state can do about it — and about the running twentieth-century argument over who should own the big network industries at all. The candidates: **privatise** (sell to the market), **nationalise** (bring into state hands), **regulate** (leave ownership alone, police behaviour), and **promote competition** (change the game so power can't persist). The exam's favourite twist: for many industries, *ownership matters less than the degree of competition and the quality of regulation*. ## Privatisation **Privatisation** transfers state-owned assets or industries to the **private sector** — the UK's sales of BT, British Gas and the railways are the textbook wave; dozens of countries (including Egypt's ongoing state-enterprise sell-offs) have followed. **The case for:** the **profit motive** attacks X-inefficiency (private owners hunt costs the state tolerated); exposure to competition and the **takeover threat** disciplines managers; investment decisions escape short-term political interference; the sale raises one-off revenue; wider share ownership. **The case against:** if the industry is a **natural monopoly**, a private owner just swaps public inefficiency for private *exploitation* (higher prices, P far above MC); social objectives — universal service to unprofitable villages, safety margins, environmental care — may be dropped because they don't pay; revenue is one-off but profits leave forever; and 'efficiency' gains sometimes arrive as job cuts and lower quality. ## Nationalisation The reverse move — taking industries into **state ownership** — is defended where the market's verdict is unacceptable: control of **natural monopolies** in the public interest, guaranteed **universal service**, capture of positive externalities (rail reduces road congestion), strategic security (energy grids), and coordination economies. Its chronic diseases: weak incentives → **X-inefficiency**, political meddling in pricing and investment, a soft budget constraint (loss-making state firms rarely die — a moral-hazard problem), and the taxpayer's open wallet. ## The natural monopoly problem — why regulation exists A **natural monopoly** has economies of scale so vast that **LRAC falls over the entire relevant market**: one water network is cheaper than two. Breaking it up *raises* costs — so the choice is regulate it, own it, or let it gouge.
**What each part means:** the yellow **LRAC** falls across the whole market — the more customers the network serves, the cheaper each one becomes — with **MC** (orange) below it (whenever average cost is falling, marginal cost must be under it). If a regulator imposes the allocatively efficient price **P = MC** (orange dot at **Qmc, Pmc**), price sits *below* average cost — the shaded band between **AC** and **Pmc** is a **loss on every unit**. So regulators must either subsidise MC-pricing, allow **average-cost pricing** (P = AC, normal profit, break-even), or cap prices — pure market forces can't deliver both efficiency and survival here.
**The regulator's toolkit:** - **Price-cap regulation ('RPI − X')** — prices may rise by inflation *minus* an efficiency factor X, forcing the firm to find cost savings and hand them to consumers. Sharp incentives — but set X wrong and you get windfall profits (too loose) or starved investment (too tight). - **Rate-of-return / profit regulation** — cap the profit rate on capital. Gentler, but invites 'gold-plating' (over-investing to inflate the capital base). - **Quality standards and performance targets** — otherwise price-capped firms cut quality instead of costs (leaky pipes, late trains). - **Yardstick competition** — benchmark regional monopolies against each other to pierce the information fog. **The regulator's diseases:** **information asymmetry** (the firm knows its true costs; the regulator negotiates in the dark) and **regulatory capture** — years of close contact, industry-funded expertise and revolving-door careers can turn the watchdog into a lapdog. ## Competition policy Where competition is *possible*, the state's job is to keep it alive: **merger control** (block combinations creating excessive power), **anti-cartel enforcement** (price-fixing and collusion, punished with heavy fines), action against **abuse of dominance** (predatory pricing to bankrupt entrants), **deregulation** and lowering entry barriers to make markets **contestable**, plus consumer-protection law. The contestability insight from Unit 7 applies: sometimes the cheapest 'regulation' is simply making entry easy. ## Evaluation — the organising judgement Ask three questions of any industry. **Is it a natural monopoly?** If yes, competition can't work — regulate or own it. **Can competition be created?** If yes (telecoms after technology broke the old network monopoly), privatisation *plus* liberalisation beats both state ownership and heavy regulation. **How good is the state at regulating?** Capture-prone, information-poor regulators weaken the case for private-plus-regulated. Hence the theme: **ownership matters less than competition and regulatory quality** — the UK's privatised utilities behave as differently as their regulators allow.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Privatisation**: profit motive vs X-inefficiency, depoliticised investment, revenue — but natural-monopoly exploitation, lost social goals. - **Nationalisation**: public-interest control, universal service, externalities — but X-inefficiency, political meddling, soft budgets. - **Natural monopoly**: LRAC falls over whole market; **P = MC makes a loss** → subsidy, average-cost pricing, or price caps. - **RPI − X** caps: strong efficiency incentives; X too tight → underinvestment, too loose → windfalls. Watch **quality-cutting**. - Regulator problems: **information asymmetry**, **regulatory capture**; mitigations: yardstick competition, standards. - **Competition policy**: merger control, anti-cartel, anti-predation, deregulation → contestability. - Master judgement: *competition where possible, regulation where not — ownership itself is secondary*.

keyTerms.definition: Transferring ownership of assets or industries from the public to the private sector.
Bringing privately-owned industries into state ownership.
An industry where economies of scale make a single supplier the lowest-cost provider.
Limiting a monopoly's price increases to below inflation to force efficiency gains to consumers.
When a regulator comes to act in the interest of the firms it regulates rather than consumers.
Government measures to promote competition and prevent abuse of market power.
Removing barriers to entry and rules to increase competition and contestability.

diagrams: **Natural monopoly:** a downward-sloping LRAC over the whole market with demand; show that one firm minimises cost, and mark the loss-making point if forced to price at P = MC (justifying regulation/subsidy).
**Monopoly vs regulated price:** compare the profit-max (MC=MR) price with a regulated price closer to allocative efficiency.
tips: Argue that **the degree of competition and regulation often matters more than ownership** — a strong evaluative line.
Use the **natural monopoly diagram** to justify why some industries are regulated rather than broken up.
Explain **RPI − X price caps** and their weakness: **regulatory capture** and **information asymmetry**.
Give **balanced privatisation/nationalisation** cases (efficiency & revenue vs externalities & consumer exploitation).
Bring in real examples (**UK privatisations of BT, British Gas and rail; water regulation by Ofwat; or China's state-owned enterprises**) for application.

channels.name: tutor2u Economics
channels.why: Clear on privatisation, regulation and competition policy.

revisionNotes:
# Privatisation, nationalisation & regulation — quick revision

**Privatisation (public → private)**
- For: profit motive cuts X-inefficiency; takeover threat; no political meddling; sale revenue.
- Against: private **natural monopoly** exploits consumers; social objectives (universal service) dropped; one-off revenue; job/quality cuts.

**Nationalisation (private → public)**
- For: natural-monopoly control, universal service, positive externalities, strategic industries.
- Against: X-inefficiency, political interference, soft budget constraint (moral hazard), taxpayer cost.

**Natural monopoly**
- LRAC falls over entire market (MC below AC) → one firm cheapest; break-up wasteful.
- **P = MC → loss** (price below AC) → choose: subsidised MC pricing, average-cost pricing (P = AC, break-even), or price caps.

**Regulation tools**
- **RPI − X price cap** — efficiency incentive; calibration risk (investment vs windfalls).
- Rate-of-return cap — gentler; 'gold-plating' risk.
- Quality standards/targets — stop quality-cutting under price caps.
- **Yardstick competition** — compare regional monopolies.
- Diseases: **information asymmetry**, **regulatory capture**.

**Competition policy** — merger control; cartels/price-fixing banned; predatory-pricing action; deregulation → **contestability**; consumer law.

**Judgement** — competition where possible, regulation where not; **ownership < competition + regulatory quality**. Examples: UK BT/gas/rail & Ofwat; state-owned enterprises reform in China/Egypt.
questions:
# Practice questions — Privatisation, nationalisation & regulation **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 3 style — multiple choice **1.** An industry is a natural monopoly if: - A — the government has granted it a legal monopoly - B — long-run average cost falls over the entire range of market demand - C — it owns a natural resource - D — it makes supernormal profit
▸ Show answer

B. Scale economies are so large that one firm supplies the whole market at lower cost than two or more could — the cost structure, not law or resources, defines it.

**2.** Under RPI − X price-cap regulation, the value of X represents: - A — the rate of inflation - B — the efficiency gain the firm must pass on to consumers - C — the firm's profit margin - D — the exchange rate
▸ Show answer

B. Prices may rise by inflation (RPI) minus X — so the firm must cut real unit costs by at least X or lose profit, transferring efficiency gains to consumers.

**3.** A regulator that increasingly makes decisions favouring the industry it supervises, after years of close contact and lobbying, illustrates: - A — yardstick competition - B — regulatory capture - C — the principal–agent problem in reverse - D — nationalisation
▸ Show answer

B. The watchdog comes to serve the watched — through information dependence, lobbying and revolving-door careers.

**4.** Which is the strongest **economic** argument for privatising a state-owned airline that faces several competitors? - A — the government gains permanent annual revenue - B — competition plus the profit motive should reduce X-inefficiency - C — air travel is a public good - D — privatisation guarantees lower fares
▸ Show answer

B. With real competition, private ownership sharpens cost discipline. A is wrong (sale revenue is one-off), C misuses the term, and D overclaims — nothing is guaranteed.

## Paper 4 style — data response > Twenty years after a country privatised its water industry into regional monopolies overseen by a price-cap regulator, a review finds: bills up 40% in real terms; dividends consistently high; leakage targets missed in 12 of 20 years; and executives moving freely between the companies and the regulator. Campaigners demand renationalisation; the industry blames tight price caps for underinvestment; the treasury notes renationalisation would cost \$90 billion. **(a)** Define 'price-cap regulation'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain why water is usually considered a natural monopoly. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse **two** reasons why price-cap regulation may have failed to protect consumers in this case. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether renationalisation is the best remedy available to this government. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Limiting a regulated firm's price rises to inflation minus an efficiency factor (RPI − X). [1+1]

(b) Enormous fixed network costs (pipes, treatment) → LRAC falls over the whole market → duplicating networks would raise costs; one supplier is cheapest → natural monopoly. [4]

(c) (i) **Regulatory capture**: revolving-door careers suggest the regulator internalised industry interests → caps set loose (high dividends alongside missed targets); (ii) **information asymmetry**: firms overstate costs → generous caps; also quality neglect — price caps without enforced leakage standards let firms cut maintenance. Any two developed. [3+3]

(d) For renationalisation: aligns objectives with public interest, ends dividend leakage, cheap state borrowing. Against: \$90bn opportunity cost; state record on efficiency; the diagnosis points at the regulator, not ownership — tougher caps, enforced quality targets, capture safeguards (cooling-off periods), or licence revocation may fix it for a fraction of the cost. Judgement: strengthen regulation first; renationalise as last resort. [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain why a natural monopoly cannot simply be broken up into competing firms, and how a regulator can control it instead. **[8]** **(b)** Assess the view that the performance of an industry depends more on the degree of competition it faces than on whether it is publicly or privately owned. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Falling LRAC → break-up raises costs; P = MC pricing loss problem; tools: price caps (RPI − X), rate-of-return caps, quality standards, yardstick competition.

(b) For: competitive private airlines vs monopoly private water — conduct tracks competition; state firms in competitive markets can perform well; contestability disciplines regardless of owner. Against: ownership shapes objectives (universal service) and investment horizons; soft budgets blunt state firms even amid competition. Judgement: competition (or its threat) is usually decisive, with regulation quality a close second; ownership matters most where both are absent.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain the main tools a competition authority can use to promote competition and protect consumers. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether privatisation necessarily improves economic efficiency. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Merger control, cartel prohibition & fines, abuse-of-dominance action (predatory pricing), deregulation/entry promotion, consumer protection — with examples.

(b) For: profit motive → productive/X-efficiency gains (evidence from many UK sales); dynamic gains where competition exists. Against: natural monopolies → allocative harm without strong regulation; social objectives lost; short-termism; efficiency gains from competition, not ownership per se. Judgement: improves efficiency conditionally — market structure and regulation decide.
